facts.text: Millwood’s name comes from a mill that operated on Echo Lake, giving the hamlet its current identity.
The original Millwood station built in 1888 burned shortly after opening, replaced by a baggage car until a new station was moved there in 1910.
Passenger service at Millwood station ended May 29, 1958, when the New York Central discontinued the Putnam Division due to low ridership.
The Millwood Fire Company began after a 1924 railroad station fire, with residents raising funds for a Brockway chemical pumper.
Sarles' Tavern, also called Granite House, was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1979 for its 19th‑century roadside hospitality.
Millwood emerged in the late 18th century as Sarlesville, a modest hub amid scattered farms owned by the Sarles family.
